message.content: Adversaries may exfiltrate data in fixed-size chunks or limit packet sizes to avoid detection by network monitoring systems. This technique, known as Data Transfer Size Limits, involves breaking down large files into smaller segments to circumvent data transfer threshold alerts. Various threat actors and malware families employ this method. For instance, APT28 splits archived files into chunks smaller than 1MB, while Carbanak compresses data into chunks if a message exceeds 4096 bytes. Tools like Cobalt Strike and Rclone also support chunking to facilitate stealthy data exfiltration. DNS-based exfiltration methods, such as those used by Helminth and Kessel, involve splitting data into small pieces that fit within DNS queries. Mitigation strategies include monitoring for unusual data transfer patterns, implementing data loss prevention (DLP) solutions, and setting up alerts for abnormal network traffic behaviors. Regularly updating and patching systems can also help reduce the risk of exploitation.
